senses.definition: An old scientific name for a group of simple worms with long, narrow bodies and a special feeding tube, now usually called nemerteans.
examples: Older textbooks may still use the term Rhynchocoela for ribbon worms.
The class Rhynchocoela is now more commonly referred to as Nemertea in modern zoology.

senses.definition: Relating to or belonging to the former worm group Rhynchocoela (nemerteans).
examples: The researcher compared rhynchocoelan and annelid body structures.
These fossils show features typical of rhynchocoelan worms.

senses.definition: A worm that belongs to the group Rhynchocoela (nemerteans).
examples: Each rhynchocoelan in the sample was measured and preserved.
The rhynchocoelan is characterized by an extensible proboscis.

senses.definition: A fluid-filled cavity in some worms that holds and supports the proboscis.
examples: The rhynchocoele allows the proboscis to extend rapidly during feeding.
The size of the rhynchocoele varies between different nemertean species.

senses.definition: Relating to or having a rhynchocoele, a cavity that contains the proboscis in some worms.
examples: The rhynchocoelic system plays an important role in locomotion.
Rhynchocoelic structures are characteristic of many nemertean worms.

senses.definition: Having or characterized by a rhynchocoele, the proboscis cavity found in certain worms.
examples: Rhynchocoelous worms possess a long eversible proboscis.
The species was described as rhynchocoelous with a well-developed proboscis.

senses.definition: Plural of rhynchocoele, fluid-filled cavities that hold the proboscis in some worms.
examples: In these species, the rhynchocoels extend almost the full length of the body.
The arrangement of the rhynchocoels differs between the two genera.

senses.definition: Having beak-like or snout-like teeth.
examples: The fossil fish had a distinctly rhynchodont arrangement of its front teeth.
Rhynchodont structures suggest a specialized feeding habit.

senses.definition: A fossil remain of the hard beak of an ancient cephalopod, such as an ammonoid.
examples: Rhyncholite fossils are often found separate from the animal's shell.
The museum display included several rhyncholites from the Jurassic period.

senses.definition: An extinct genus of marine brachiopods with ribbed, triangular shells, common in Jurassic and Cretaceous rocks.
examples: Rhynchonella is frequently recorded in Late Jurassic marine deposits.
The fossil was identified as a species of Rhynchonella based on its shell shape.

senses.definition: An old or less-used name for a superfamily or related group of brachiopods that includes forms similar to Rhynchonella.
examples: Some older classifications still mention Rhynchonellacea as a distinct superfamily.
The Rhynchonellacea were once considered a major group of articulate brachiopods.

senses.definition: A family of articulate brachiopods known from the Ordovician to recent times.
examples: Rhynchonellidae includes many fossil brachiopods with strongly ribbed shells.
The family Rhynchonellidae is widespread in Paleozoic marine rocks.

senses.definition: Resembling or related to brachiopods of the genus Rhynchonella.
examples: The shell shows a distinctly rhynchonelloid outline.
These rhynchonelloid features suggest a close relationship to Rhynchonella.

senses.definition: A large group of beetles commonly known as weevils, usually with the head extended into a snout.
examples: The Rhynchophora include many serious agricultural pests.
Insect keys often treat Rhynchophora as a distinct group within the beetles.

senses.definition: A small plant genus of woody vines in the family Malpighiaceae, native to Madagascar.
examples: Rhynchophora is a Malagasy genus with unusual winged fruits.
Only two species of Rhynchophora are currently recognized.

senses.definition: Relating to or belonging to the beetle group Rhynchophora (weevils).
examples: Rhynchophoran beetles can cause heavy damage to stored grains.
The study focused on rhynchophoran morphology and evolution.

senses.definition: A beetle that belongs to the group Rhynchophora (a weevil).
examples: Each rhynchophoran in the sample was identified to species.
The rhynchophorans were trapped using pheromone lures.

senses.definition: In some animals, a structure that bears or supports a snout or proboscis.
examples: The rhynchophore is highly developed in this group of marine worms.
Damage to the rhynchophore can prevent the animal from feeding.

senses.definition: Plural of rhynchophore, structures that bear or support snouts or proboscises in some animals.
examples: The paired rhynchophores are visible in dorsal view.
Different species show various forms of rhynchophores.

senses.definition: Having a beak or snout, or relating to the beetle group Rhynchophora.
examples: The rhynchophorous head is adapted for piercing plant tissue.
These rhynchophorous insects are key pests of palm trees.

senses.definition: A subfamily within a bird family, including species of the genus Rhynchops (skimmers).
examples: Rhynchopinae are noted for their unusual bill shape.
The Rhynchopinae are sometimes grouped with terns in field guides.

senses.definition: A bird genus that includes skimmers, which have a lower bill longer than the upper one and feed by skimming the water surface.
examples: Rhynchops species often feed at dusk along estuaries.
The black skimmer, Rhynchops niger, is common along the Atlantic coast.

senses.definition: A plant genus in the pea family (Fabaceae), including various climbing or trailing legumes.
examples: Rhynchosia species are often found in dry, open habitats.
Some Rhynchosia are used as forage plants in tropical regions.

senses.definition: A large genus of sedge-like plants, often called beak-rushes, that grow in wet places.
examples: Rhynchospora is especially diverse in tropical wetlands.
Several Rhynchospora species are indicators of bog habitats.

senses.definition: An old taxonomic name once used for a group of insects with piercing mouthparts, now usually called Hemiptera.
examples: Early entomologists sometimes treated Rhynchota as a separate order.
The term Rhynchota has largely been replaced by Hemiptera in modern classifications.

senses.definition: Relating to or belonging to Rhynchota, an old name for a group of insects with piercing mouthparts.
examples: Rhynchotal forms were long recognized as a distinct order of insects.
The rhynchotal mouthparts are adapted for sucking plant sap.

senses.definition: An insect belonging to the former order Rhynchota, roughly equal to what are now called Hemiptera (true bugs).
examples: The fossil was described as a rhynchote based on its piercing beak.
Several rhynchotes were collected from the marsh vegetation.

senses.definition: Having a beak-like or snout-like structure, especially in reference to insects with piercing mouthparts.
examples: The rhynchotous mouthparts allow the insect to pierce tough plant tissues.
Rhynchotous forms are common among sap-sucking insects.

senses.definition: Belonging to or resembling the brachiopod family Rhynchonellidae.
examples: Rhynconellid shells are typically strongly ribbed.
The fauna is dominated by rhynconellid brachiopods.

senses.definition: A brachiopod that belongs to the family Rhynchonellidae.
examples: The limestones are rich in rhynconellids and corals.
Each rhynconellid in the sample was identified to species.

senses.definition: A group or section of birds characterized by having a beak-like mouth opening.
examples: The Rhyncostomi were distinguished by their long, narrow bills.
Some older classifications refer to a group called Rhyncostomi.

senses.definition: In western England, a man-made water channel or ditch used for drainage.
examples: The fields are crossed by a network of rhynes.
He fell into a rhyne while walking across the moor.

senses.definition: Plural of rhyne, drainage ditches or channels, especially in parts of England.
examples: The rhynes must be cleared regularly to prevent flooding.
Cattle grazed in fields bordered by rhynes.

senses.definition: An extinct genus of very simple early land plants from the Devonian period.
examples: Rhynia is often cited as a classic early vascular plant.
Fossils of Rhynia were first discovered in the Rhynie Chert of Scotland.

senses.definition: An extinct family of simple early land plants that includes the genus Rhynia.
examples: Rhyniaceae are important for understanding the evolution of vascular plants.
The family Rhyniaceae is known mainly from the Rhynie Chert deposits.

senses.definition: A bird genus that includes the kagu, a rare, ground-dwelling bird from New Caledonia.
examples: Rhynocheti is represented today by a single living species, the kagu.
The genus Rhynocheti is endemic to New Caledonia.

senses.definition: A Dutch typeface or printing style used in early modern books.
examples: The text was set in an old rhynsburger type.
Collectors value early rhynsburger imprints for their historical charm.

senses.definition: An igneous rock with a composition between rhyolite and basalt.
examples: The volcano erupted lavas ranging from rhyobasalt to andesite.
Thin sections show rhyobasalt with both mafic and felsic minerals.

senses.definition: A volcanic rock that is intermediate in composition between rhyolite and dacite.
examples: The dome is made mostly of rhyodacite.
Rhyodacite lavas can produce explosive eruptions.

senses.definition: A light-colored volcanic rock rich in silica, often linked with explosive eruptions.
examples: The cliff is formed of pale rhyolite.
Rhyolite eruptions can create large ash deposits.

senses.definition: Plural of rhyolite, light-colored volcanic rocks rich in silica.
examples: These mountains are built mainly of rhyolites and andesites.
The region shows a wide variety of rhyolites.

senses.definition: Made of or relating to rhyolite, a light-colored, silica-rich volcanic rock.
examples: The rhyolitic magma produced an explosive eruption.
Rhyolitic domes grow slowly as thick lava piles up near the vent.

senses.definition: Showing a banded or streaked texture in volcanic rock because crystals and bits of glass are aligned by flow.
examples: The rhyotaxitic texture indicates strong flow during solidification.
Rhyotaxitic lavas show streaks of aligned crystals.

senses.definition: A writer or painter who focuses on low, vulgar, or very ordinary subjects.
examples: Critics dismissed him as a mere rhyparographer.
The novel's rhyparographer delights in the dirt and gossip of city life.

senses.definition: Writers or painters who specialize in low, vulgar, or very ordinary subjects.
examples: The rhyparographers of the period enjoyed mocking high society.
Greek artists sometimes spoke of rhyparographers who painted humble scenes.

senses.definition: Describing art or writing that deals with low, vulgar, or very ordinary subjects.
examples: His rhyparographic style shocked polite readers.
The painting has a distinctly rhyparographic focus on street life.

senses.definition: Works of art or writing that focus on low, vulgar, or very ordinary subjects.
examples: His early rhyparographies were rejected by publishers.
The museum held an exhibition of eighteenth-century rhyparographies.

senses.definition: A person who creates writing or art about low, vulgar, or very ordinary subjects.
examples: The rhyparographist found inspiration in crowded taverns.
As a rhyparographist, she preferred market scenes to noble portraits.

senses.definition: The depiction of low, vulgar, or very ordinary subjects in art or writing.
examples: Rhyparography flourished in certain periods of Greek painting.
The novel's vivid rhyparography exposes the city's underclass.

senses.definition: A variant term for rhyparography, the depiction of low or vulgar subjects in art or writing.
examples: Rhypography was looked down on by some classical critics.
The painter's rhypography focused on taverns and markets.

senses.definition: Obscure, hidden, or difficult to understand.
examples: The poem is written in a highly rhyptic style.
His notes were so rhyptic that even he could not read them later.

senses.definition: Having the nature of rhyptic language; obscure or hard to interpret.
examples: Her rhyptical remarks puzzled the audience.
The text grows increasingly rhyptical towards the end.

senses.definition: An instrument used to measure the flow or seepage of water through soil or rock.
examples: The engineer installed a rhysimeter to monitor groundwater movement.
Rhysimeter readings were taken every week during the experiment.

senses.definition: A genus of ichneumon wasps whose females drill into wood to lay eggs in larvae of other insects.
examples: Rhyssa are used as natural enemies of wood-boring pests.
The female Rhyssa uses a long ovipositor to reach hidden hosts.
